comments:
Summary:
This short theoretical paper addresses an important gap in the understanding of self-paced learning (SPL). Prior work observed that the alternating optimization strategy (AOS) used to solve SPL corresponds to a majorization-minimization (MM) algorithm on an implicit objective G_λ, but could only guarantee monotonic decrease of G_λ, not convergence. The authors present a set of theorems (Theorems 1–3) proving that, under reasonable assumptions, every cluster point of the sequence produced by AOS/MM is a critical point of the implicit objective G_λ. They also allow for inexact minimization steps with summable errors. Proofs employ variational analysis tools and an extension of Zangwill’s global convergence theorem, and are presented in appendices.

Strengths:
- The problem is well-motivated: establishing convergence to critical points strengthens the theoretical foundation for using the implicit SPL objective to explain robustness properties.
- The theoretical results are natural and useful: showing convergence under mild and verifiable conditions (lsc, level-bounded/coercive regularizer, differentiability of losses, continuity of v*). The relaxation to inexact updates (ǫ_k ∈ l1) increases practical relevance.
- Proof approach is standard but appropriate (Zangwill, outer semicontinuity, variational arguments) and the lemmas are clearly stated.

Weaknesses and concerns:
- The paper is incremental: it extends existing MM/EM convergence theory to this SPL setting, rather than introducing fundamentally new techniques. The main novelty is applying those known tools to formalize the SPL-to-implicit-objective connection and explicitly allowing inexact minimizations.
- Some assumptions may be restrictive in practice (e.g., uniqueness and continuity of v*λ(·), differentiability of loss w.r.t. w, coercivity of φ_λ). The authors should discuss how these apply to common SPL instantiations (e.g., nonconvex deep models, nonsmooth regularizers) and whether the results extend.
- No illustrative examples or numerical experiments are given to demonstrate scenarios, verify assumptions, or show behavior in practice. Even minimal synthetic experiments would help readers gauge applicability.
- Presentation has minor formatting and typographical artifacts in the provided text (line breaks, reference numbering). A careful proofreading and more exposition around the assumptions and examples would improve readability.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Add a short discussion section that (i) clarifies the practical verifiability of the assumptions for commonly used models and SPL regularizers, (ii) contrasts the results with prior MM/EM convergence results more explicitly, and (iii) points out limitations (e.g., stochastic or online updates, high-dimensional nonconvex models).
- Provide at least one simple example (synthetic problem or standard SPL instantiation) illustrating convergence or showing how the error-tolerant variant behaves.
- Fix minor formatting issues and expand on connections to non-convex regularized penalties (NCRP) mentioned in the introduction.

Recommendation rationale:
The work provides a useful theoretical clarification that completes part of the SPL picture. It is solid and of interest to researchers in learning theory and optimization applied to SPL, but it is somewhat incremental and lacks empirical illustration. I therefore recommend acceptance at a moderate level (suitable for a workshop/poster or a brief conference contribution).
